category: Occupational fraud - fictitious/inflated vendor billing scheme
risk_signals: Payments to a vendor with no verifiable existence, premises, or tax footprint
Invoices sized just under an approver's authority limit, or duplicate invoice numbers
Vendor bank account linked to, or controlled by, an employee
analyst_reasoning: In a billing scheme an insider sets up or colludes with a shell vendor and approves fictitious or inflated invoices for payment. As ACFE occupational-fraud data shows, billing schemes are among the most common and costly asset-misappropriation frauds; the tells are vendor non-existence and invoices engineered to slip under approval limits.
investigation_steps: Validate vendor existence: registration, premises, tax IDs, and independent references
Compare vendor bank details against employee records for overlaps
Analyse invoices for just-under-limit sizing, duplicates, and round amounts
decision: Suspend payments to the vendor, run vendor-master and three-way-match controls, and pursue disciplinary/legal action on confirmed collusion.
outcome: Billing schemes bleed funds steadily until vendor-master controls catch them; three-way matching is the primary defence.
lessons_learned: Vendor non-existence plus an employee-linked bank account is the core billing-scheme signal
Invoices engineered just under approval limits indicate insider knowledge
Vendor-master governance and three-way match are the structural controls
